title: Ravioli di ricotta e spinaci
subtitle: Fresh pasta filled with ricotta and spinach, dressed with sage butter
excerpt: Fresh egg pasta filled with ricotta, spinach, Parmesan, and nutmeg, served with brown butter and crisp sage. The filling takes five minutes; the dough takes ten to make and thirty to rest. Rolling by hand or machine and sealing takes patience. Worth learning once, the technique works for any fresh pasta shape.
sourceNotes: Ravioli filled with ricotta and greens is documented throughout northern and central Italy from the medieval period. Artusi includes ravioli di ricotta in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) and notes the regional variation between spinach and chard as the filling green. The sage butter (burro e salvia) dressing for fresh pasta is a Lombard and Piedmontese preparation documented from the same period. The filling combination of ricotta, spinach, Parmesan, and nutmeg is the canonical form, sometimes called pasta verde in reference to the green filling.

content.text: Fresh pasta dough requires only two ingredients: flour and eggs. The difficulty is not the recipe but the feel, the dough must be worked until it is smooth, supple, and springs back slowly when pressed. Under-kneaded dough tears when rolled; properly kneaded dough can be stretched paper-thin without tearing. The resting period is not optional: it relaxes the gluten so the dough rolls out without fighting back.

The filling must be dry. Frozen spinach contains a great deal of water; squeeze it rigorously after thawing or it will make the filling wet and the ravioli will burst. The ricotta should also be drained if it is very liquid, tip it into a sieve for 30 minutes first. A wet filling is the most common cause of ravioli failure.

items.name: Plain flour
items.prepNote: tipo 00 if available; plus more for dusting

items.name: Eggs
items.prepNote: 3 for the dough, 1 beaten for sealing

items.name: Ricotta
items.prepNote: drained in a sieve for 30 minutes if very wet

items.name: Parmesan
items.prepNote: finely grated; 60 g for filling, 20 g to serve

content.text: Mound the {{plain-flour}} of flour on a board. Make a well in the centre. Crack 3 {{eggs}} into the well. Beat with a fork, gradually incorporating the flour from the inner wall of the well. Once the dough is too thick to fork, use your hands. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky. Wrap in cling film and rest at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Squeeze the thawed {{spinach-frozen}} spinach in a clean cloth until completely dry, then chop finely. Combine with the {{ricotta}}, 60 g of the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan, a generous grating of {{nutmeg}}, salt, and black pepper. Mix until smooth. Taste for seasoning.

Divide the rested dough into 4 pieces. Roll one piece on a floured surface until very thin, about 1 to 2 mm, thin enough to see the shape of your hand through. Place teaspoon-sized mounds of filling at 5 cm intervals across half the sheet. Brush around each mound with the beaten egg. Fold the unfilled half of the sheet over the filling side, pressing firmly around each mound to seal and push out air. Cut into individual ravioli with a knife or pastry cutter. Press the edges firmly to seal.

Bring a large pot of generously salted water to a boil. Cook the ravioli in batches for 3 to 4 minutes until they float and the dough is tender. Meanwhile, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide pan over medium heat until foaming and turning pale gold. Add the {{sage}} leaves and fry for 30 seconds until crisp. Drain the ravioli and add directly to the butter pan. Toss gently. Serve immediately with the remaining {{parmesan}} of Parmesan grated over.

items.fix: Squeeze the spinach completely dry; use beaten egg to seal and press firmly; boil at a gentle simmer rather than a rolling boil
items.cause: Filling too wet; or edges not sealed firmly; or boiling too vigorously

items.fix: Knead for the full 8 to 10 minutes; rest for the full 30 minutes; work in smaller pieces
items.cause: Dough under-kneaded or not rested long enough

items.fix: Use a small, heaped teaspoon of filling per ravioli; press the dough tightly around the filling to expel all air
items.cause: Too much filling; edges not pressed flat
items.symptom: Filling leaks out and the ravioli feels loose before cooking

items.fix: Keep at medium heat and watch constantly; remove from heat the moment it turns pale golden and smells nutty
items.cause: Heat too high; butter left unattended

content.text: Ricotta and spinach ravioli belongs to the broad family of magro (lean) fillings: pasta made without meat, eaten on fasting days in the Catholic calendar. It appears across northern and central Italy with small regional changes. Swiss chard instead of spinach in Liguria. Ricotta mixed with eggs in Rome. A more aggressively seasoned filling in the south. The sage butter sauce, burro e salvia, is the standard dressing for any delicate fresh pasta in Lombardy and Piedmont. The quality of the butter and the freshness of the sage are the only variables. It is one of the simplest and most effective sauces in Italian cooking.
